items.question: Write the formulas for RMSE and MAE over n examples.
items.answer: RMSE = √((1/n) Σ (yᵢ − ŷᵢ)²). MAE = (1/n) Σ |yᵢ − ŷᵢ|. Both are in the same units as y.

items.question: Why is RMSE always greater than or equal to MAE for the same predictions?
items.answer: RMSE is the square root of the mean of squared absolute values; MAE is the mean of absolute values. By Jensen's inequality / √(mean(a²)) ≥ mean(|a|), RMSE ≥ MAE, with equality only when all |residuals| are equal.

items.question: You trained with MSE loss. What metric should you report on the test set?
items.answer: Report test RMSE = √(test MSE) for interpretability in y's units. Optionally also report test MAE. Do not report training MSE/RMSE as final quality — use the held-out test split.

items.question: How do RMSE/MAE differ from MSE/MAE in Loss Functions?
items.answer: Same core formulas. Loss Functions owns MSE/MAE as training objectives minimized during fit. This topic owns RMSE/MAE (and MSE→RMSE conversion) as evaluation metrics on validation/test data for model comparison and reporting.

items.question: Two models have equal test MAE but different test RMSE. What does that imply?
items.answer: They have the same average absolute error but different tail behavior. The model with higher RMSE has larger squared errors — more occasional big misses — even though typical |error| matches.
